one_line_takeaway: A structure-aware fairness/safety/security benchmark for audio LLMs shows that Phi-4-Multimodal and Qwen2-Audio trade off differently (lower attack success but higher toxicity for Phi-4; higher attack success but lower toxicity for Qwen2), with audio-modality processing itself -- not just prompt content -- driving roughly 22-31% cross-modal safety degradation relative to text.
key_finding: Averaged across 14 unsafe categories, Phi-4 shows C-RR 49.97%, C-SRR 39.58%, C-ASR 9.91%, C-Tox 8.41%; Qwen2 shows C-RR 46.21%, C-SRR 41.88%, C-ASR 11.44%, C-Tox 2.73% -- i.e., Phi-4 has a lower attack success rate but markedly higher toxicity in the responses that do slip through (e.g., hate speech C-Tox 34.54 for Phi-4 vs. 26.90 for Qwen2; discrimination 17.30 vs. 3.51). Cross-modal safety degradation (CMSI) from text to audio input is about 31% for Qwen2 versus 22% for Phi-4. Under adversarial audio attacks on Qwen2, PGD perturbations produced larger average latency drift (+7.88) than FGSM (+3.02), and both attacks shortened average response length (PGD -78.52, FGSM -48.78 tokens/words) while semantic similarity to the clean-audio response stayed high (PGD 0.837, FGSM 0.844) and average tonality drift was modest (PGD 0.086, FGSM 0.103), indicating the models' semantic comprehension was largely preserved under attack even though response latency and length were disrupted. On fairness, both models exhibit heightened Ordinal Equalized Odds disparities for under-represented accents (Phi-4: African vs. English/American; Qwen2: Indian vs. American/Australian), and models that appear tonally neutral on benign prompts diverge under unsafe prompts -- Phi-4 trending dismissive, Qwen2 trending moralizing/normative.
core_contribution: The paper introduces (1) a structural taxonomy of audio language models (ALLMs) along two axes -- system type (cascaded ASR+LLM, audio-text multimodal, or audio-native) and input representation (discrete tokens vs. continuous embeddings) -- and (2) a unified evaluation framework that ties fairness, safety, and security (FSS) measurements to that taxonomy, so that observed model behavior can be attributed to architectural design choices rather than treated as an undifferentiated benchmark score. The framework is empirically applied to two audio-text multimodal, continuous-embedding systems, Qwen2-Audio-7B-Instruct and Phi-4-multimodal-instruct, across matched audio/text inputs.
threat_model: Security is defined narrowly as robustness to adversarial interference targeting the model's input representation or inference pipeline. The concrete instantiation is an untargeted, white-box-style adversarial audio attack: gradient-based perturbations (FGSM and PGD, budget epsilon=0.02) are generated against a surrogate ASR model (wav2vec2) and applied to the raw waveform, with the goal of degrading/destabilizing model behavior without steering toward a specific output. Perturbations are confined to the audio channel; the paired text prompt is left clean, so any behavioral shift is attributed to audio-modality vulnerability rather than semantic change. Separately, a safety threat model covers harmful/unsafe spoken requests spanning 14 categories (e.g., child abuse, terrorism, drug abuse, privacy violation), and a fairness axis probes whether paralinguistic variation (accent, emotion, gender) causes disparate safety/tone outcomes for semantically identical requests.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: the empirical study covers only two models, both audio-text multimodal with continuous embeddings -- fully cascaded and audio-native architectures from the taxonomy are not empirically tested, so the taxonomy's discriminative value across all three system types is asserted but not verified experimentally. The threat model for the security axis is limited to untargeted, single-turn, gradient-based waveform perturbations against a surrogate ASR (wav2vec2) rather than an end-to-end white-box or adaptive attack on the ALLM itself, and multi-turn interactions and richer/adaptive threat models are left to future work. Prompts are entirely TTS-synthesized rather than natural recordings, and human validation of the LLM judge was performed by a single annotator (a paper co-author) on a 150-sample stratified subset, which is a small and non-independent validation set. The paper does not report statistical significance testing on the observed cross-model differences.

quantitative_results: Phi-4 average: C-RR 49.97%, C-SRR 39.58%, C-ASR 9.91%, C-Tox 8.41
Qwen2 average: C-RR 46.21%, C-SRR 41.88%, C-ASR 11.44%, C-Tox 2.73
Phi-4 hate speech C-Tox 34.54 vs Qwen2 26.90; Phi-4 discrimination C-Tox 17.30 vs Qwen2 3.51
Qwen2 under PGD: latency drift +7.88, response-length drift -78.52, semantic similarity 0.837, tonality drift 0.086
Qwen2 under FGSM: latency drift +3.02, response-length drift -48.78, semantic similarity 0.844, tonality drift 0.103
LLM-judge vs. human agreement: overall 85.33% (N=150); Fairness 94%, Safety 82%, Security 80%
Sub-metric agreement: Comprehension 96% (all categories), Safety 68%, Tonality 64% (Security) / 92% (Fairness)
baselines_compared: Qwen2-Audio-7B-Instruct vs. Phi-4-multimodal-instruct (the two models evaluated head-to-head; no external prior benchmark is re-run as a baseline)
